questions.question: What is the recommended temperature target after cardiac arrest if TTM is chosen?
questions.rationale: If TTM is chosen, the 2025 AHA guidelines recommend selecting and maintaining a constant temperature between 32‚Äď37.5¬įC for at least 36 hours. Maintaining ‚Č§37.5¬įC is the minimum standard regardless of the TTM approach.

questions.question: When must neuroprognostication be performed after cardiac arrest?
options: C. ‚Č•72 hours after ROSC (or after rewarming)
questions.rationale: Neuroprognostication must be deferred to ‚Č•72 hours after ROSC (or after rewarming if TTM was used), to allow clearance of sedation, paralysis, and metabolic derangements that confound the examination.

questions.question: What is the MAP target after ROSC?
questions.rationale: MAP ‚Č•65 mmHg (SBP >90 mmHg) is the hemodynamic target. Both hypotension (MAP <60) and excessive hypertension (MAP >100) worsen neurological outcomes.

questions.question: What percentage of cardiac arrest survivors may have STEMI on post-ROSC ECG?
questions.rationale: Approximately 25‚Äď50% of cardiac arrest survivors have acute STEMI on post-ROSC 12-lead ECG, making immediate ECG acquisition critical.

questions.question: What is the target ETCO‚āā range during normothermic ventilation after ROSC?
questions.rationale: Target ETCO‚āā is 35‚Äď45 mmHg. Both hyperventilation (ETCO‚āā <35) and hypoventilation (ETCO‚āā >45) worsen cerebral perfusion after cardiac arrest.

questions.question: What are the indications for ECPR?
options: A. All cardiac arrests regardless of cause
B. Selected refractory VF/pVT, low-flow time <60 minutes, reversible cause, suitable candidate
D. All arrests with downtime >30 minutes
questions.rationale: ECPR is indicated for selected refractory cardiac arrest (persistent VF/pVT despite standard ACLS), expected low-flow time <60 minutes, potentially reversible cause, and suitable candidates.

questions.question: Why should neuroprognostication NOT be performed before 72 hours?
options: A. Because the brain has not been injured yet
B. Because sedation, paralytics, metabolic derangements, and hypothermia confound the neurological examination
C. Because 72 hours is arbitrary and has no evidence
D. Because EEG is not available before 72 hours
questions.rationale: Multiple factors confound the neurological exam before 72 hours: sedation and paralytics depress consciousness, metabolic derangements alter exam findings, and hypothermia affects neurological function. Early exams are unreliable.

questions.question: What is the preferred first-line vasopressor for hemodynamic support after ROSC?
questions.rationale: Norepinephrine (starting 0.1‚Äď0.5 mcg/kg/min, titrated to MAP ‚Č•65) is the preferred first-line vasopressor. Dobutamine may be added for myocardial dysfunction.

questions.question: What is the maximum recommended rewarming rate after TTM?
questions.rationale: Rewarming should be slow and controlled at ‚Č§0.25¬įC per hour to prevent rebound intracranial hypertension, hemodynamic instability, and metabolic derangements.

questions.question: What NSE (neuron-specific enolase) level at 48‚Äď72 hours suggests poor neurological prognosis?
questions.rationale: NSE >33 mcg/L at 48‚Äď72 hours post-ROSC is associated with poor neurological outcome, but must be interpreted within the multimodal prognostication framework ‚ÄĒ no single test is sufficient.

questions.question: Which clinical examination findings at ‚Č•72 hours post-ROSC suggest poor neurological prognosis?
options: A. Bilateral pupillary reflexes present, purposeful motor response
B. Bilateral absent pupillary and corneal reflexes with absent motor response to pain
C. Unilateral absent pupillary reflex only
D. Mildly sluggish but present reflexes
questions.rationale: At ‚Č•72 hours, bilateral absent pupillary and corneal reflexes combined with absent motor response to painful stimuli (or extensor posturing) are indicators of poor prognosis ‚ÄĒ but must be confirmed with multimodal assessment.

questions.question: What is the minimum temperature standard after cardiac arrest if TTM is NOT chosen?
questions.rationale: Even if TTM at 32‚Äď37.5¬įC is not chosen, the minimum standard is maintaining temperature ‚Č§37.5¬įC with continuous monitoring and immediate treatment of any fever.

questions.question: What imaging finding on post-arrest CT suggests severe hypoxic-ischemic brain injury?
options: B. Diffuse cerebral edema or loss of gray-white matter differentiation
C. Single small vessel territory infarct
questions.rationale: Diffuse cerebral edema or loss of gray-white matter differentiation on CT indicates severe hypoxic-ischemic brain injury and is associated with poor neurological prognosis.

questions.question: ECPR involves which type of circulatory support?
options: B. Veno-arterial ECMO (VA-ECMO) via femoral cannulation
C. Impella left ventricular assist device only
D. Peripheral cardiopulmonary bypass with cardiac cooling
questions.rationale: ECPR typically uses veno-arterial (VA-ECMO) with femoral venous drainage and femoral arterial return, placed percutaneously under ultrasound guidance. A distal perfusion catheter prevents limb ischemia.

questions.question: What is the ECMO weaning approach?
options: B. Gradual reduction guided by echocardiographic recovery, hemodynamic stability, and ETCO‚āā trends
C. Weaning is not possible ‚ÄĒ ECMO is permanent
D. Weaned based on time only, regardless of cardiac function
questions.rationale: Weaning from ECMO is guided by echocardiographic recovery of native cardiac function, hemodynamic stability on decreasing support, and clinical assessment ‚ÄĒ performed in a monitored setting with resuscitation equipment available.

questions.question: What does ECPR represent in the treatment pathway ‚ÄĒ a bridge or a destination therapy?
options: B. A bridge to further therapy (PCI, TTM, native cardiac recovery)
C. An alternative to standard ACLS
D. A last resort with no further treatment options
questions.rationale: ECPR is a bridge ‚ÄĒ it provides temporary circulatory support while the reversible cause of arrest is identified and treated. It is weaned as native cardiac function recovers.

questions.question: Which multimodal approach is used for neuroprognostication after cardiac arrest?
options: B. Clinical exam + SSEPs + EEG + NSE/S-100B + neuroimaging
questions.rationale: Neuroprognostication requires a multimodal approach combining clinical examination, electrophysiology (SSEPs, EEG), serum biomarkers (NSE, S-100B), and neuroimaging (CT/MRI). No single modality is sufficient alone.

questions.question: When should rehabilitation planning begin for a cardiac arrest survivor?
options: B. Only after neuroprognostication is complete
C. Early in the ICU with passive range of motion and mobilization; formal referral before discharge
D. Rehabilitation is not part of cardiac arrest care
questions.rationale: Rehabilitation begins early in the ICU with passive range of motion, upright positioning, and mobilization. Formal referral to cardiac rehabilitation, neurorehabilitation, and psychosocial services should be initiated before discharge.
